topics: What are fractions and fractional units
subject: Mathematics

sections.heading: ⚔️ The Quest Begins — What is a Fraction?
sections.body: Welcome, young wizard! Imagine you have one roti and you want to share it equally between 2 friends. Each friend gets half — and we write that as 1/2. That is a fraction! A fraction tells us how much of a whole thing one person or one part gets. The bottom number (denominator) tells you how many equal pieces the whole is cut into, and the top number (numerator) tells you how many pieces you have. So 3/4 means the whole was cut into 4 equal parts and you are holding 3 of them — like eating 3 out of 4 pieces of a gulab jamun!
keyPoints: A fraction shows a part of a whole — like 1/2 roti or 3/4 of a chikki
The bottom number (denominator) = total equal parts; the top number (numerator) = your share
More people sharing the same roti means each person gets a smaller piece — so 1/9 is less than 1/5!

sections.heading: 🧙 Spell of the Fractional Unit — Equal Parts of a Whole
sections.body: Every brave wizard must master the fractional unit — it is the smallest equal piece you get when you cut a whole into parts. When you break a chikki bar into 6 equal pieces, each piece is 1/6 of the whole chikki. These tiny equal pieces are called fractional units or unit fractions. Think of a cricket pitch divided into equal zones — each zone is a fractional unit of the whole pitch! Now here is the power spell: fractions like 3/4 simply mean you have collected 3 of those tiny 1/4 pieces together.
keyPoints: A fractional unit is one equal part of a whole — like 1/4, 1/6, or 1/8
A fraction like 5/8 means you have taken 5 pieces of the fractional unit 1/8
Fractions have been used in India since ancient times — 3/4 was called 'tri-pada' in the Rig Veda!

sections.heading: 🗺️ The Realm of the Number Line — Placing Fractions
sections.body: Now your quest takes you to the magical number line realm! Draw a line from 0 to 1 — that gap is exactly 1 whole unit. If you cut that gap into 4 equal parts, each tiny part is 1/4 of the unit. You can mark 1/4, 2/4, and 3/4 between 0 and 1. Fractions whose numerator is smaller than the denominator — like 2/5 or 3/7 — always live between 0 and 1 on the number line. Fractions whose numerator is bigger than the denominator — like 7/4 — are heroes that have crossed past 1 and live beyond it!
keyPoints: On the number line, the space from 0 to 1 can be split into equal fractional parts
If numerator is smaller than denominator (like 3/5), the fraction is less than 1
If numerator is bigger than denominator (like 7/4), the fraction is greater than 1

sections.heading: 🐉 Defeating the Dragon — Mixed Fractions
sections.body: Some fractions are so powerful they are bigger than 1 whole! These are called mixed fractions and they have two parts — a whole number and a fraction. For example, if you eat 1 full paratha and then half of another, you ate 1 and 1/2 parathas — written as 1½. We can also write this as 3/2 because 1 whole equals 2/2, and 2/2 + 1/2 = 3/2. Going the other way, to turn a mixed number like 2⅔ back into a fraction, multiply the whole number by the denominator and add the numerator — so 2⅔ becomes (2×3+2)/3 = 8/3. You have now defeated the mixed fraction dragon!
keyPoints: A mixed fraction = a whole number + a proper fraction, like 2¾ (two and three quarters)
To convert a mixed number to a fraction: multiply whole number × denominator, then add the numerator

sections.heading: ✨ The Master Spell — Equivalent Fractions
sections.body: A true fraction wizard knows that different fractions can point to the exact same amount — these are called equivalent fractions! Think of sharing a pizza. If you cut it into 2 parts and take 1 piece, you get 1/2. If the same pizza is cut into 4 parts and you take 2 pieces, you still get the same amount — that is 2/4. So 1/2 and 2/4 are equivalent fractions, they are equal! You can find equivalent fractions by multiplying both the top and bottom number by the same number. For example, 1/3 = 2/6 = 3/9 — they are all the same size, just cut differently, like the same amount of mango juice poured into different sized glasses.
keyPoints: Equivalent fractions look different but represent the same amount — like 1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6
Multiply or divide both numerator and denominator by the same number to find equivalent fractions
A fraction wall (paper strips) is a great way to see which fractions are equal in length

summary: Young wizard, you have completed the Fractions Quest! You learned that a fraction shows a part of a whole — like sharing a roti equally among friends. The bottom number tells you how many equal parts the whole is cut into, and the top number tells you how many parts you have. You explored fractional units (tiny equal pieces), placed fractions bravely on the number line, conquered mixed fractions that are greater than 1 whole (like 2¾ parathas!), and mastered the powerful spell of equivalent fractions — discovering that 1/2, 2/4, and 3/6 all mean exactly the same thing. You now have the power and mastery of fractions — well done, hero!
